message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/fastspeech-new-text-to-speech-model-improves-on-speed-accuracy-and-controllability/
**Title:** FastSpeech: TTS model with length-regulator speaking-rate control (Microsoft Research)
**Found via:** rate-aware-TTS/fine-tune-vs-timestretch search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract claims about: (1) speaking-rate / speed control in TTS — how FastSpeech's length regulator lets you control voice speed/duration and whether speech stays intelligible/clear when sped up or slowed down; (2) any quantitative numbers on inference speed, speedup factor, MOS quality, word skipping/repeating error rates; (3) how FastSpeech controls prosody/pitch and voice speed natively at generation time vs post-hoc time-stretching; (4) the publish date of the article. Quote the exact wording for each claim including any percentages or numbers.

content.content:
# FastSpeech Claims Extraction

## (1) Speaking-Rate/Speed Control

**Duration Control Capability:**
"FastSpeech can adjust the voice speed through the length regulator, varying speed from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality."

**Prosody Control:**
"can also change the duration of blank tokens to adjust the break between words in order to control part of the prosody."

The article indicates speed variations maintain intelligibility but doesn't provide explicit data on clarity degradation at extreme speeds.

## (2) Quantitative Performance Numbers

**Inference Speedup:**
"FastSpeech speeds up the mel-spectrogram generation by about 270 times and speeds up the end-to-end audio synthesis by about 38 times."

**Robustness (Word Errors):**
"Transformer TTS is not robust when it comes to these hard cases and gets a 34% error rate, while FastSpeech can effectively eliminate word repeating and skipping."

**Voice Quality (MOS):**
"FastSpeech nearly matches the quality of the Transformer TTS model and Tacotron 2" (exact MOS values provided in Table 1 but not quoted in text).

## (3) Native vs. Post-hoc Speed Control

FastSpeech controls speed natively at generation time through the length regulator mechanism, not via post-hoc time-stretching. The duration predictor determines phoneme durations during synthesis.

## (4) Publication Date

"Published December 11, 2019"

content.text: I have sufficient information to extract the claims.

claims.claim: FastSpeech's length regulator enables native control of speaking rate from 0.5x to 1.5x speed at generation time (not post-hoc time-stretching) without loss of voice quality, by scaling predicted phoneme durations.
claims.quote: FastSpeech can adjust the voice speed through the length regulator, varying speed from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality.

claims.claim: FastSpeech can control part of the prosody, specifically inter-word pause/break length, by adjusting the duration of blank tokens at generation time.
claims.quote: can also change the duration of blank tokens to adjust the break between words in order to control part of the prosody.

claims.claim: Native duration-prediction generation eliminates word repeating and skipping errors that plague autoregressive TTS; on hard cases Transformer TTS has a 34% error rate while FastSpeech effectively eliminates them.
claims.quote: Transformer TTS is not robust when it comes to these hard cases and gets a 34% error rate, while FastSpeech can effectively eliminate word repeating and skipping.

claims.claim: FastSpeech achieves large inference speedups: ~270x faster mel-spectrogram generation and ~38x faster end-to-end audio synthesis, supporting feasibility of controllable non-autoregressive TTS for local/fast pipelines.
claims.quote: FastSpeech speeds up the mel-spectrogram generation by about 270 times and speeds up the end-to-end audio synthesis by about 38 times.

claims.claim: Despite non-autoregressive generation with explicit duration control, FastSpeech's voice quality nearly matches autoregressive Transformer TTS and Tacotron 2 (MOS), showing rate-controllable generation need not sacrifice naturalness.
claims.quote: FastSpeech nearly matches the quality of the Transformer TTS model and Tacotron 2

content.text:
Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from the Microsoft Research FastSpeech blog post (published 2019-12-11), rated as a primary source (institutional research blog from the originating lab).

Key findings relevant to the research question's angle (3) on rate-aware/controllable TTS:
- The length regulator provides native generation-time speaking-rate control (0.5x–1.5x) by scaling predicted phoneme durations, rather than post-hoc time-stretching — directly relevant to the fine-tune-vs-timestretch trade-off.
- It also controls inter-word pause length via blank-token duration, touching the pause/rhythm angle.
- Native duration-based generation eliminates the word skipping/repeating errors (34% error rate baseline) that degrade autoregressive TTS, a robustness argument for native fast generation.

Note: the blog presents qualitative "without loss of voice quality" claims but does NOT provide measured intelligibility/comprehensibility gains at extreme speeds (the article tops out at 1.5x, far below the 18–22 syllables/sec power-listener regime), so it does not quantify the "significant gain" bar the user cares about.
